topic: Using Object-Oriented Programming to create emergent networks of interacting nodes

concepts.name: Node Class (Autonomous Agents)
concepts.explanation: Instead of just drawing points, the instructor creates a 'Node' class where each instance manages its own properties like position (x, y), speed (xSpeed, ySpeed), and color.
concepts.why_it_matters: It allows you to treat individual elements as independent entities that have their own behavior and state.

concepts.name: Constructor
concepts.explanation: A special function within a class used to initialize new objects with specific properties like random starting positions and speeds.
concepts.why_it_matters: It's the blueprint that defines what every new object created from a class will look like and how it starts.

concepts.name: Boundary Collision Logic
concepts.explanation: Using conditional statements to check if a node's position has hit the edge of the canvas, and then multiplying its speed by -1 to reverse direction.
concepts.why_it_matters: This keeps the objects contained within the visible area and creates a 'bouncing' effect.

concepts.name: Passing Arrays to Methods
concepts.explanation: A method inside a class (like drawLines) can take an entire array of other objects as a parameter so the individual object can 'look' at its neighbors.
concepts.why_it_matters: This is how you move from individual, isolated objects to a system where objects interact and form relationships.

concepts.name: Emergent Geometry
concepts.explanation: Using the distance between nodes to decide whether to draw a line or a shape, creating complex patterns (like constellations) from simple rules.
concepts.why_it_matters: It demonstrates how complex, organic-looking structures can emerge from very simple mathematical rules applied to many individual agents.

code_examples.demonstrates: The basic structure of a Node class including properties and movement logic.
code_examples.common_issue: Forgetting to use 'this.' when accessing class properties inside methods.

code_examples.demonstrates: How a node can iterate through an array of other nodes to draw lines between itself and nearby neighbors.
code_examples.common_issue: Forgetting to pass the 'nodes' array into the method call.

mistakes_and_fixes.cause: Trying to iterate over a variable that hasn't been passed into the function correctly.
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Ensure you pass the array as an argument when calling the method: `n.drawLines(nodes)`.

mistakes_and_fixes.cause: The `color` property was assigned a simple number (like from `random()`) instead of a p5.js color object.
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Initialize the property using `color(r, g, b)` in the constructor so it is a proper color object.

mistakes_and_fixes.cause: Changing the object's property (like `this.color`) immediately before using it in a drawing function, which affects subsequent calls.
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Be careful with the order of operations when modifying object properties like alpha.

key_moments.description: The instructor notes that nodes are initially 'unaware' of each other, creating the tension needed to justify passing an array into a method.
Explaining $O(n^2)$ complexity: as you add more nodes, the number of calculations increases exponentially because every node checks every other node.
The instructor highlights the 'neat trick' of passing an array to a method, allowing objects to interact without needing global variables.

student_questions: How do I make my shapes interact with each other?
Why are all my circles just moving randomly and not connecting?
How do I make a line appear only when two points are close together?
Why is my code saying 'is not a function' when I try to change the color alpha?
How do I make objects bounce off the edges of the screen?
Why is my program getting slow when I add more objects?
How do I pass a list of things into a class method?
My shapes are all in one spot, how do I make them spread out?
How can I use the mouse to trigger something in my class?
What is a constructor used for in a class?
